data.title: How China’s history of fighting fire reflects a long and difficult struggle
data.description:
It will be a sombre December for Hong Kong as the city recovers from the shock of the massive fire that engulfed seven high-rise residential blocks in Tai Po last week, the deadliest fire in Hong Kong in decades.
At the time of writing, 159 people are confirmed dead and dozens more are injured. Few would remain unmoved by the distraught tears of victims who lost family members and their homes to the blaze.
Fire was a constant threat in premodern Chinese cities, where most buildings were made of wood and built with tightly joined timber frames.
The very qualities that made these structures beautiful and utilitarian also made them dangerously flammable. Dense roof structures trapped heat and smoke, and good ventilation – often necessary for comfort – fed flames instead of stopping them.
Unsurprisingly, successive dynasties invested enormous effort in preventing fires.
Some measures were purely symbolic. Perched on the ends of roof ridges, for example, could be found decorative wenshou: mythical water creatures believed to ward off evil and, in keeping with their aquatic nature, fire.

Similarly, the names of buildings were considered important, especially those housing precious, highly flammable items like books. When the Complete Library of the Four Treasuries, a massive encyclopaedia commissioned by the Qing dynasty’s Qianlong Emperor, was completed in 1782, copies were housed in seven imperial libraries, six of which featured the radical for “water” in their names. The seventh was excluded because it stood on the banks of the Yangtze River.
Alongside symbolic precautions came more concrete methods. As early as the Tang dynasty (618-907), records describe water-filled animal skin bags that were squeezed by several strong men to create rudimentary high-pressure jets to put out fires.
By the Northern Song (960-1127), firefighting technology had become slightly more hi-tech. Sources from the period list water bladders made from animal hides or the stomachs of pigs or cattle, manually operated pumps using perforated bamboo tubes, and “hemp mops” – long poles weighted with water-soaked hemp and mud – that were used to beat back flames.
During his time governing the city of Hangzhou, Su Shi (1037-1101) even established an official fire brigade equipped with ropes, axes, saws, signal flags and protective clothing.
During the Ming dynasty (1368-1644), firefighting became more institutionalised. Major urban centres maintained proper fire stations stocked with buckets, ladders and rudimentary hoses.
The imperial palaces of the Ming and Qing (1644-1912) dynasties were guarded by elite troops who doubled as fire wardens; by the time of the early Qing, Beijing’s Forbidden City had a specialised fire brigade made up of several hundred men. Their main tool was the “water dragon” (shuilong), a hose connected to a sophisticated hand-operated pump.

The most visually striking fire-prevention measure of all was static, as seen in the hundreds of massive water vats positioned throughout the Forbidden City. Each held over 3,000 litres (800 gallons) of water and required daily upkeep. In summer, palace eunuchs ensured the water stayed clean, and in winter, they wrapped the vats in quilted covers and kept charcoal fires glowing beneath to prevent freezing.
In the late Qing, foreign technology began to supplement traditional equipment. In 1887, Hangzhou bought a Western-style wheeled pump, nicknamed the “foreign dragon” (yanglong), and engaged a dedicated crew to operate it. By the 1890s, local authorities owned several such machines, alongside ladders, ropes and axes.
Most of these past measures were, however, largely ineffective, especially in large, heavily built-up cities.
Hong Kong’s recent tragedy is a grim reminder that fires are an enduring menace. From symbolic safeguards to modern fire safety regulations, China’s long history of fire prevention and firefighting reflects a constant struggle to confront the destructive force that still haunts cities today.

data.title: The enduring legacy of bamboo, a symbol of resilience, strength and adaptability in Asia
data.description:
Bamboo, a subfamily of typically fast-growing, tall, tree-like grasses, is found in tropical, subtropical and mild temperate regions across almost all continents, except Antarctica and Europe. In prehistoric South, East and Southeast Asia, it is well known for its use in tools, weapons, means of construction, transport and food.
Due to its characteristics of bending without breaking and remaining evergreen, bamboo symbolises resilience, strength and adaptability, as well as prosperity and fertility. The ancient Chinese poetry collection Book of Songs contains numerous references to bamboo symbolising family prosperity.
Europeans’ encounters with the plant and its name during their age of exploration are first attested in the writings of Portuguese doctor, herbalist and naturalist Garcia de Orta, who worked primarily in Goa and Bombay in Portuguese India.
In his 1563 Coloquios dos Simples, the earliest European treatise on the medicinal and economic plants of India, he writes of “aquellas canas daquella arvore chamam os Indios, onde nasce, mambu”, describing “the canes of that tree the Indians, where it grows, call mambu”.
Similarly, bamboe is documented in the 1598 writings of Dutch merchant traveller J.H. van Linschoten, as (in the English translation) “a thicke reede, as big as a mans legge, which is called Bambus”.

While some point to Sumatran Malay, Sundanese and Javanese bambu, or an old Malay word samambu, as the source of this word in European languages, many accounts concur that the ultimate origin lies in the Dravidian language Kannada bănbŭ or banwu.
“Bamboo” lends itself to several concepts, given its strong associations with East Asia.
The “bamboo curtain” – coined by analogy to Europe’s Iron Curtain – refers to the political and economic barrier, from 1949 to 1972, between communist territories of East Asia, particularly the People’s Republic of China, and non-Communist countries.
The “bamboo network” conceptualises the vast constellation of businesses owned and run by overseas Chinese in Southeast Asia – notably the Min/Hokkien communities – which has flourished for more than a century, tracing back to the mass migrations of Chinese labourers and traders during the Qing dynasty (1644-1912) and the colonial period. Built on familial ties, trust and values, the network links the region to the economies of Greater China.
The “bamboo ceiling” is an offshoot of the “glass ceiling”, both metaphors for systemic barriers in the workplace.
The “glass ceiling”, coined in the 1970s, refers to the invisible barrier that prevents women from advancing to higher positions because of their gender. Extending this concept, the “bamboo ceiling”, coined in 2005, references the combination of individual, cultural and organisational factors that impede the career progress of Asian-Americans, particularly in reaching senior leadership positions.

In Chinese languages, the concept of bamboo – zūk (竹) in Cantonese, or zhū in Mandarin – generally evokes positive traits such as steadiness and resilience.
An interesting exception is found in Cantonese, where a term with more negative connotations exists: zūk sīng (竹升). The term refers to a bamboo pole, describing someone who is more Western than Chinese, due to having been born or raised or having spent a significant amount of time overseas and being devoid of Chinese culture and values, much like a hollow bamboo pole.
The original term for a bamboo pole was actually zūk gong (竹杠). However, the second character is homophonous with the character gong (降), which has the potentially inauspicious meaning of “fall” or “decrease”. Thus, this character was replaced with its opposite: sīng (升), meaning “rise”.
Bamboo scaffolding – zūk pàahng (竹棚) in Cantonese – has been made a scapegoat by some commentators for the devastating fire that razed Wang Fuk Court in Hong Kong’s Tai Po district last week.
The practice of bamboo scaffolding, which dates back millennia in China, has long been the most iconic of construction materials and practices in Hong Kong, characterising the city’s urban landscape.
Its particular use for constructing temporary Cantonese opera stages – Bamboo Theatre Building Technique – was inscribed on the Hong Kong Representative List of Intangible Cultural Heritage in 2017.

data.title: Why the Philippines can’t escape its endless cycle of disaster
data.description:
The tarpaulin barely held against the wind, sagging under days of ceaseless rain.
For the earthquake survivors of San Remigio, in the central Philippines, it was all that remained – a thin sheet of plastic above a patch of mud, standing in for the homes a 6.9-magnitude quake had reduced to rubble weeks earlier.
Then, in early November, Typhoon Kalmaegi barrelled through.
The storm, known locally as Tino, drenched thousands of families still huddled in their makeshift shelters. Roads vanished beneath floodwater. Power lines snapped and stayed down. Aid was stranded on the wrong side of washed-out highways. For many, hope felt as precarious as the tarpaulin pinned above their heads.

“One piece of tarpaulin the size of a plywood sheet for a whole family, that was all they had,” said Mayfourth Luneta, deputy executive director of the Center for Disaster Preparedness. “If you have five family members, you’re basically just standing under it.”
Luneta had flown from Manila to central Cebu province after the quake, only to find herself trapped in the chaos of a second disaster. Kalmaegi struck just as her team was due to deliver relief. The main highway out of Mandaue City turned into a river.
“People were shocked because that area doesn’t usually flood like that, not to the extent we were hearing about,” she said.
When her team finally found a way through, they discovered communities pushed past the point of desperation. Some had been without electricity for weeks. Others lined the sides of roads, begging for help. “Everywhere we went, they kept saying, ‘Thank you so much! We’ve been without electricity for so long!’”
We’re seeing three typhoons ravage the country in a week
Mayfourth Luneta, Filipino aid worker
For aid workers like Luneta, what is unfolding in the Philippines no longer feels like a series of isolated emergencies. It feels like a punishing new normal.
“We used to see one or two strong typhoons a year. Now it feels like three in a week,” she said.
This convergence of back-to-back storms, worsening floods and overlapping disasters offers a stark preview of life on the front lines of global warming, scientists say. Disasters that once seemed rare and extraordinary now crash into one another, erasing the space between recovery and the next emergency.
For communities already struggling with poverty, weak infrastructure and systemic corruption, each shock piles onto the last, creating the sense of a seemingly endless cycle of emergencies.

In Marikina City, which endures serious flooding almost every year, Philippine Red Cross administrator Jennifer de Guzman is accustomed to the grim routine of disaster response.
“But the situation in Cebu during Typhoon Tino felt different,” said the aid worker who joined a 10-day relief operation after the storm had hit.
“Even though we were familiar with the rhythm of emergency operations, the sheer scale of the destruction in Cebu demanded more from us: more coordination, more manpower, more time.”
The damage spanned a wide area, forcing a bigger, more agile response. In Marikina, years of repeated floods have bred local know-how and hard-won resilience, but Cebu showed how even seasoned responders can become overwhelmed when multiple overlapping disasters arrive all at once.


Super typhoons such as Haiyan in 2013, one of the most destructive tropical cyclones ever recorded, were once treated as once-in-a-generation calamities.
“But now, we’re seeing three typhoons ravage the country in a week,” Luneta said. “We have been feeling the worsening effects of climate change on disasters in the Philippines first hand.”
“It wasn’t this severe before … From the neglect we’ve shown in the destruction of the environment, we are now harvesting this in heaps of disasters,” she told This Week in Asia.
Clusters of cyclones
Scientists and front‑line responders warn that climate change is dramatically altering the types, frequency and severity of disasters hitting the Philippines.
Gwendolyn Pang, secretary general of the Philippine Red Cross, said the changes were now unmistakable. “There has been a notable increase in both the frequency and intensity of these tropical cyclones,” she told This Week in Asia.
Last year, the Southeast Asian nation saw a rare cluster of six cyclones hit in less than five weeks. This year, two super typhoons struck within days of each other.
And typhoons are only part of the story. Regions that were once protected from major flooding are now being hit by heavy monsoon rains and unexpected storms, putting extra pressure on relief teams.

Pang said the country’s geography of scattered islands made relief work all the more difficult, so agencies were now focusing on preparing for disasters before they happened.
The Philippines has weathered a succession of powerful typhoons in recent years, including Bopha in 2012, Haiyan in 2013 and Rai in 2021.
New climate studies are beginning to quantify what communities have long sensed. Researchers at Imperial College London’s Grantham Institute have found that climate change intensified the destructive power of Kalmaegi and Fung‑wong – typhoons known locally as Tino and Uwan that struck within days of each other last month, killing more than 250 people.
Human-induced climate change had increased both wind speeds and rainfall, making storms of this magnitude “more frequent, dangerous and costly because of the continued use of fossil fuels”, said institute director Ralf Toumi.
Lessons from Japan
The Philippines sits squarely in the Pacific Typhoon Belt and the West Pacific Cyclone Basin, two of the world’s most disaster-prone zones where extreme weather is common.
Japan faces similar dangers from storms and earthquakes, but has been able to lower its risk through better planning and preparedness. The Philippines ranks as the country with the highest disaster risk globally, while Japan is ranked 17th, according to the 2025 World Risk Index published by Germany’s Development Aid Alliance and the Institute for International Law of Peace and Armed Conflict.
“This shows that risk is not the same as hazard. Risk must be managed,” said geologist Mahar Lagmay, a professor at the University of the Philippines.

“If you can manage the exposure of people and communities, control development and strengthen the capacities of the population, you can lower the risk.”
“Japan is far more effective at this,” he added, citing the East Asian nation’s advanced planning, ample resources and proven ability to minimise risks despite also facing severe hazards, including snow-related dangers.
“Japan’s greater resources allow it to reduce vulnerability,” Lagmay said. “The Philippines, by contrast, has far less capacity.”
Despite living with typhoons, floods and earthquakes as a matter of course, the Philippines is still ill prepared for the scale of devastation now routinely unfolding, experts say.
We are trapped in a cycle of merely preparing, but we can no longer truly defend ourselves
Ven Paolo Valenzuela, Filipino researcher
“We are trapped in a cycle of merely preparing, but we can no longer truly defend ourselves against disasters,” said Ven Paolo Valenzuela, a Filipino research fellow at Singapore Management University who specialises in disaster risk reduction and climate change adaptation.
“It’s as if we’re just waiting to get punched and we were never taught how to dodge.”
For Valenzuela, the clearest example of this gap is the country’s inability to pass a national land-use law – a basic framework for deciding where people should live, where businesses and infrastructure can safely be built, and which areas are simply too dangerous for permanent settlement.
“Land-use classification is absolutely critical for a risk-informed development pathway,” he said. “Which areas are exposed to natural hazards? Which are safe? Which areas should have residents because they are safe, and which can we afford to develop with some calculated risk?”

Without such a framework, even the best disaster-risk data could not meaningfully shape how cities expanded or where roads, bridges and industrial zones were sited, Valenzuela added.
Since Haiyan struck in 2013, the Philippines has made progress in sharing hazard information and raising public awareness. But much of the scientific knowledge is still seen as academic research rather than being used in everyday planning and decision-making.
One of the most ambitious efforts was Project Noah, short for Nationwide Operational Assessment of Hazard, which was launched in 2012 to provide detailed, real-time hazard maps and warnings for evacuations and rescue efforts.
Noah combined global science and advanced technology such as Lidar 3D scanning to map risks, model floods and identify danger zones. Scientists credit it with helping to cut the Philippines’ weather-related deaths by almost two-thirds between 2014 and 2024.

But Noah was shut down in 2017 due to funding cuts, moving to the University of the Philippines in a much reduced role, focusing on helping local governments make science-based plans.
The loss of national-scale innovation, especially the Lidar programme, was deeply felt – had it continued, the country could have mapped far more, built stronger disaster resilience and potentially saved more lives, said Lagmay, who was the project director.
“We could have done so much more … we could have probably saved more lives,” he said. “We could have built an army of disaster researchers. We could have inspired more. We could have used more technologies.”
Billions washed away
Compounding the Philippines’ vulnerabilities is a problem that predates climate change but now magnifies its toll: corruption.
A widening flood-control corruption scandal has seen numerous lawmakers and local officials accused of colluding with contractors to build substandard, overpriced or outright non‑existent “ghost” projects, including infrastructure meant to protect communities from the very storms that are now arriving with growing ferocity.
Since 2022, the administration of President Ferdinand Marcos Jnr has disbursed a total of 545 billion pesos (US$9.2 billion) for flood-control projects. Yet Metro Manila and nearby areas still spent days submerged under deep, foul-smelling floodwaters following torrential rains this year.
Valenzuela traced the rot back decades, pointing to corruption in flood-control and related infrastructure in the 1990s and 2000s, when weak dykes were built after the deadly eruption of Mount Pinatubo in 1991.

“What makes it worse now is that climate change is delivering more intense hazards every year,” he said. “For every peso stolen, the equivalent cost is hundreds or thousands more in disaster damage, lost lives and lost futures.”
For Luneta, the effect is painfully visible on the ground, where responders are forced to stretch limited relief across an ever denser calendar of emergencies.
“We’re all fighting for meagre resources,” she said, adding that people had started to connect “the corruption issues … to the devastation they experience each year.”
Valenzuela drew a stark contrast between the Philippines and its regional peers. He slammed the country’s flood control projects as patchwork efforts that failed to match the level of risk they were supposed to manage.
He urged Philippine officials to look to neighbours such as Taiwan, Japan and Singapore, which treat disaster‑prevention works as core development infrastructure, not optional add‑ons.
“When they invest properly in coastal defences and flood control, even a strong typhoon becomes just another rainy day,” he said.
“Because our infrastructure keeps failing us … We are left to fend for ourselves and prepare on our own every single time.”

data.title: Trump talks trade with Canada, Mexico leaders at World Cup draw
data.description:
US President Donald Trump met with the leaders of Mexico and Canada to discuss trade issues on Friday after the leaders hosted the 2026 World Cup draw at Washington’s storied Kennedy Centre.
The White House said Trump held talks with Canadian Prime Minister Mark Carney and Mexican President Claudia Sheinbaum after the star-studded event, but gave no details on their discussions.
Canadian media said the meeting lasted for 45 minutes and the leaders spoke on their own, with no staff present.
Trump was upbeat about the talks before the meetings, saying trade and immigration would be on the agenda.
“We’re going to meet with both, and we’re getting along very well,” Trump told reporters as he arrived for the event.

Trump chatted with Carney and Sheinbaum as they watched one of the biggest events in the sporting world from a box at the Washington arts venue, after the trio appeared on a game show-like stage together to “draw” their individual countries’ names.
Trump, who has campaigned aggressively this year for a Nobel Peace Prize, was given an inaugural peace prize by Fifa for his efforts to promote dialogue and de-escalation in some of the world’s biggest hotspots.
The meeting came as the United States, Mexico and Canada prepare for a mandatory 2026 formal review of the US-Mexico-Canada trade agreement amid rising US tariffs and calls by Washington to renegotiate or possibly withdraw from the pact.
It was Sheinbaum’s first in-person meeting with Trump since she took office in October last year.
Trump has been broadly complimentary of the Mexican leader while pushing the country to do more to combat drug cartels and the flow of narcotics such as fentanyl to the United States.
He has vowed to maintain a 25 per cent US tariff on Mexican goods that are not compliant with the USMCA, pending more progress in the drug fight.

Relations have been more tense with Canada.
Trump had an amicable meeting with Carney in October, but then suspended all trade talks with Canada after the province of Ontario aired an advertisement using video of former US president Ronald Reagan warning that tariffs lead to trade wars and economic disaster.
Trump also threatened to increase duties on Canadian imports, though he has yet to follow through.
USMCA, which replaced the North American Free Trade Agreement in 2020 and was negotiated during Trump’s first term as president, requires the three countries to hold a joint review after six years.
Trump could decide next year to withdraw from the trade agreement, Politico reported on Thursday, citing US Trade Representative Jamieson Greer.
Greer did not repeat the comment at an event later on Thursday, telling an interviewer that USMCA was the law of the land, a law passed by Congress, and that Canada and Mexico were the United States’ largest export destinations.

data.title: What is stress? New mother finds out the hard way as experts advise on managing it
data.description:
For Hong Kong psychoanalyst Diego Busiol, the word “stress” does not adequately reflect the body and mind’s reaction to a perceived pressure.
“The term tends to flatten complex human phenomena into something overly generic,” he says. “It’s much more nuanced than that. It affects people differently.”
He gives an example: “You can have two managers with identical workloads: one develops chronic insomnia, the other thrives. The difference lies not in the pressure itself, but in each person’s history – in their ideals, beliefs, and the unconscious meaning they give to effort and responsibility.”
Stress is also not inherently bad.
“When it has direction and meaning, it can be a catalyst for growth, learning and creation. Think of someone starting an enterprise full of hope and curiosity. The physiological arousal they feel isn’t pathology – it’s enthusiasm,” Busiol says.

If “stress” aligns with a person’s desire, he explains, “it mobilises them”. On the other hand, if it is not in sync with their wants and direction, “it immobilises”.
“One type fuels movement, the other freezes it,” he says, adding that this explains why some people feel alive under pressure while others struggle.
Asha, who did not want to disclose her surname, knows all about positive stress. A high-flying marketing executive, she was headhunted for a position in Hong Kong and moved to the city in 2013.
She thrived. If work pressure began to overwhelm her, she went running. “I am that Indian who cannot meditate,” she laughs.
She was married in 2015, and “life was really good, on track”.
When you are young, Asha says, or before you have had to endure much stress, there is the feeling that it will simply pass.
“I’ve always taken traditional routes – eating healthy food, working out – because, innately, your body knows this stops it, so I will do this more. Coming from a traditional family, we weren’t taught stress management.”

Busiol says common stress-relief techniques like breathwork, meditation, yoga and mindfulness can be helpful on the surface, because they calm the body. But “they risk becoming ways to tolerate an unsustainable situation rather than to transform it”.
A year into her married life, Asha was diagnosed with cancer. Her oncologist gave her great advice to help her cope with the years of treatment that followed.
“He told me to imagine my treatment programme was a tree; we were going to tackle one branch at a time. It meant I never had to look too far ahead to ‘what ifs’ or ‘what nows’.”
Despite the challenges, she welcomed the arrival of a baby in 2021 and continued to perform well at work. Only when her husband succumbed to alcoholism soon after they became parents did she realise she could not cope.
“I was taking care of a job, of a new baby, of a husband in the throes of alcoholism because I thought it was my duty – but nobody was taking care of me.”

For her, “I should” had replaced “I want”. That, Busiol says, is when symptoms appear:
“[Problems arise] when someone acts against their own values or desires, doing things ‘because they must’ or ‘because it’s the right thing to do’,” he says. “The body becomes the stage where unconscious conflict can speak.”
Asha could not sleep or eat, and no amount of running could help. On the verge of a breakdown, she saw a therapist, who helped her understand that she could not help her husband – he had to do that. She could only help herself.
Hong Kong clinical psychologist Michael Beckham refers to this as a crisis.
“When an individual enters my office, reeling from a recent trauma – such as death, betrayal or a diagnosis – their world has just collapsed, and they fear they may collapse as well.
“At that point, I neither interpret nor instruct; I only become the singular individual in their life unafraid of the discomfort they experience.”

In a safe and objective space for the client, Beckham witnesses “panic, the tears, the fury”. With time and support, they realise “they can endure this hour, and subsequently the next. The crisis diminishes in its capacity to characterise them.”
Asha mirrors the sort of people Busiol typically sees.
“I rarely meet a ‘stressed person’,” he says. “I see someone whose life or work has lost subjective resonance. They keep going out of duty, identification or sacrifice – and at some point, the body starts speaking where meaning has collapsed.”
That could take the form of depression or insomnia, like Asha’s.
Part of the language of stress includes the activation of cortisol, adrenaline and sympathetic arousal – so-called somatic symptoms. “The body says what the person cannot,” Busiol explains.
Asha’s psychologist helped her “triage” the problem one day at a time, she says, just as her oncologist had.
“Don’t look at what could happen – that won’t get you anywhere,” Asha says her oncologist advised her. “Just see the two options in front of you. You have this or this. You either go here and afterwards we talk those options, or we go here and we talk these options.”

Her therapist did the same, she says, “and it clicked”.
Asha wanted the enormous pressures to go away, but she also understood she needed to feel control over how she managed the stress.
Beckham says the key is to focus on what you can change, such as your thoughts, intentions and deeds. The most effective strategy for handling pressure is to “respond rationally rather than emotionally”.
Asha understood that circumstances had to change for her feelings to change. She quit her job and stopped believing that she had control over her husband’s illness. She was getting better on her own, and it was up to him to do the same.
She is now in what she describes as a renaissance as a happy, well mother. She and her husband are separated, but he is better than he was. They co-parent their child amicably.
She plans to spend six months pivoting to a new career.
“I’m in hibernation, getting ready to start the next phase of my life. Let’s see where the wind takes me.”
Like what you read? Follow SCMP Lifestyle on Facebook, X and Instagram. You can also sign up for our eNewsletter here.

data.title: Hong Kong must build on foundations laid at successful Legal Week
data.description:
High-quality legal services are integral to Hong Kong’s role as a “superconnector” for China and the international community. To establish itself as a global leader in the field, there is a need for the city to constantly engage and forge new ties, so that knowledge can be shared, lessons learned and progress made. This is the aim of Hong Kong Legal Week, an annual international conference organised by the Department of Justice, which concluded on Friday. The five-day summit brought together lawyers, judges, academics and officials from multiple jurisdictions, as well as representatives of prestigious international organisations. The theme, fittingly, was “linking laws, bridging worlds”.
A wide range of issues was discussed, including developments in dispute resolution, private international law and the 35th anniversary of the Basic Law. There was a focus on current trends and technological advances, from the regulation of digital assets to the application of artificial intelligence. Hong Kong must be at the forefront of these developments, helping to standardise rules across borders and ensuring that the benefits of new technology are maximised and risks limited. With its traditions, the law can often lag behind the rapid march of technological innovation.
The conference also featured the first Greater Bay Area Lawyers’ Forum, underlining the need for closer legal links between Hong Kong, Macau and Guangdong while respecting each region’s different system. Secretary for Justice Paul Lam Ting-kwok, speaking at the opening, highlighted Hong Kong’s strong legal foundations and international connectivity, prime assets in the city’s drive to facilitate legal and economic collaboration. Hong Kong’s unique legal system, grounded in common law, provides a familiar and trusted environment for businesses and investors. The local legal profession, meanwhile, offers a variety of first-class services.
To play a central role, the city must be proactive and continue to innovate. The launch of the world’s first centre for mediation between governments in October was a landmark. This is Beijing’s initiative, but the decision to locate it in Hong Kong is significant.
The city is the most popular seat of arbitration in the Asia-Pacific and the second in the world after London. Hong Kong’s academy for training international legal talent, meanwhile, has just celebrated its first anniversary. There are exciting opportunities for growth, with a strong demand for legal services in the Greater Bay Area and among countries involved in China’s Belt and Road Initiative. Legal Week provided a valuable platform for international engagement and exchange. The foundations laid must be built on if Hong Kong is to make its mark on global legal developments.

data.title: Chinese EV maker Li Auto enters crowded AI glasses field with cheapest offering on market
data.description:
Chinese electric vehicle maker Li Auto has launched its first wearable product, the Livis artificial intelligence glasses, marking a bold foray into the rapidly expanding market already crowded by tech giants like Alibaba Group Holding, Huawei Technologies and Xiaomi.
The device is priced at 1,999 yuan (US$282), but buyers who place an order before December 31 qualify for a 15 per cent rebate via the government-backed subsidy programme, bringing down the price to 1,699 yuan.
That effectively makes it among the cheapest in China, comparable with Huawei Eyewear 2, launched in 2023 by telecoms giant Huawei Technologies, which starts at 1,699 yuan. Alibaba’s Quark G1, introduced last month, and the Xiaomi AI Glasses released in June, are both currently priced at a discounted 1,899 yuan. Alibaba owns the Post.
Li Auto’s new product, announced on Wednesday, is also among the lightest on the market, weighing 36 grams excluding the lenses.
Despite its lightweight build, Livis promises robust performance with an impressive battery life of 18.8 hours under normal usage scenarios, and up to 78 hours without use, outperforming rivals.

The Livis supports smart features similar to its rivals, including taking photos, videos and recording conversations. Notably, it facilitates voice interactions with Li Auto vehicles, such as asking the car to open the trunk.
The company is also planning an update in the first half of 2026, which will introduce enhanced AI capabilities such as “summoning a vehicle”, where owners can ask the car to drive itself out of a parking spot.
The carmaker teamed up with optical giant Zeiss to bring high‑quality lenses to the line-up. There is even an online prescription service that can produce and ship custom lenses the same day after eye test results are uploaded.
Li Auto’s foray into smart glasses marks the latest effort from a Chinese company to navigate the smart wearable market. Over the past several months, Alibaba, Xiaomi and Baidu have all announced new products in the space. Baidu’s Xiaodu AI Glass Pro, rolled out last month, weighs 39g without lenses and is priced at 2,299 yuan.
The entry of major tech firms to the sector represented efforts to find the “third screen” after laptops and smartphones, said Owen Liu Chuang, a senior researcher from Beijing-based tech research firm Runto.
“Stagnation in the global smartphone market, combined with technological breakthroughs in AI and hardware, has paved the way for this new category”, he said, adding that AI glasses were increasingly viewed as having the potential to offer “a more natural, hands-free interaction with AI”.
IDC China analyst Ye Qingqing said smart glasses were seen as “the next-generation gateway for intelligent interaction, possessing multi-modal perception capabilities through voice and vision”, with the potential to deliver AI services directly to wearers.
Analysts pointed out that companies of different backgrounds brought distinct advantages to the market. Internet giants like Alibaba and Baidu could showcase their AI strength by integrating glasses with their apps, while smartphone brands like Huawei and Xiaomi tend to leverage their mobile ecosystem, according to Liu.
Li Auto could transfer some complex computations off to its in-car chips, allowing the glasses to be ultra-lightweight while still delivering computational processing capabilities comparable to high-end smartphones, Liu said.
IDC’s Ye said Li Auto’s advantage was in its “comprehensive coverage of mobility scenarios”, allowing it to “achieve functional synergy between vehicle and glasses platforms”.
The company is not the only Chinese carmaker to come out with glasses. Last year, Nio launched the Air AR Glasses priced at 2,299 yuan, but new stock has sold out online.

data.title: Silver lining in China’s ageing population shouldn’t be overlooked
data.description:
Amid concern about China’s ageing and shrinking population, a positive factor can be overlooked. Mainlanders are living longer. Average life expectancy hit a record 79 years in 2024, after achieving the goal of growing by 0.2 years annually during the 14th five-year plan. National Health Commission head Lei Haichao says that by the end of the 15th five-year plan in 2030, the country’s newborns should be expected to live an average of 80 years, in line with President Xi Jinping’s “Healthy China” blueprint.
Average life expectancy in 2015 was 76.3 years. Raising it to 79 over the ensuing 10 years was a huge achievement, given China’s population and the socio-economic challenges of the Covid-19 pandemic.
At the same time, the population has declined for three consecutive years to 2024, for the first time in decades, and more than 21 per cent, or about 300 million people, are over 60 years of age.
China is now ranked 10th for life expectancy among the Group of 20 countries and outperforms others in the same income basket. It is one of the very few that reports improvements every year. This is largely attributable to a combination of investment in public healthcare, poverty alleviation programmes and improved health awareness among the population.
Looking ahead, rising life expectancy presents numerous opportunities for China as well as the challenges of a huge ageing population. Artificial intelligence and robotics are transforming the workforce landscape, enabling people to extend their working years. While automation may displace certain jobs, it also raises demand for elderly care services.
This shift leads to a growing investment in facilities and infrastructure catering to the ageing population, often referred to as the “silver economy”.
Moreover, the development of China’s biomedicine industry is poised for expansion, as advancements in healthcare complement the needs of an ageing demographic. It is good that the government is also focusing on promoting sports and active lifestyles among the general populace, aiming not just for longer lives but healthier ones.

data.title: Trump’s security playbook recasts US allies as tools to counter China: analysts
data.description:
US President Donald Trump’s new national security strategy frames America’s allies not just as partners, but as instruments to preserve US primacy and counter China’s rise, analysts say.
The strategy urges wealthy allies to increase defence spending, align more closely with Washington on measures such as export controls, and take greater responsibility for their regions, a shift that recasts long-standing alliances as tools in great-power competition.
The 33-page document declares that the era of the US “propping up the entire world order like Atlas” is over.
“The United States must work with our treaty allies and partners – who together add another $35 trillion in economic power to our own $30 trillion national economy (together constituting more than half the world economy) – to counteract predatory economic practices,” it states.
Its goal is explicit: “to help safeguard our prime position in the world economy and ensure that allied economies do not become subordinate to any competing power.”

It adds, “We will also work to align the actions of our allies and partners with our joint interest in preventing domination by any single competitor nation.”
The strategy identifies China as a near-peer competitor and emphasises US efforts to counter its economic, technological and military influence.
Experts see this as a dramatic shift. In a social media post, Zack Cooper of the American Enterprise Institute highlighted a “big shift” in the “language on allies”, observing that “allies are depicted as a means to an end”.
He emphasised the centrality of China in the strategy, saying it remains the “biggest focus in Asia (and elsewhere)” and that the “Trump team [is] clearly seeing all of Asia through the lens of China”.
Cooper also highlighted omissions, noting, “Asia gets the most pages, but nearly all are about China. US treaty ally Philippines isn’t even mentioned! ... Pacific Islands are also nowhere to be seen.”
The national security strategy’s broader message to allies is uncompromising: “We count among our many allies and partners dozens of wealthy, sophisticated nations that must assume primary responsibility for their regions and contribute far more to our collective defence.”
The strategy also lays out clear incentives. Nations that “willingly take more responsibility for security in their neighbourhoods and align their export controls with ours” will receive “more favourable treatment on commercial matters, technology sharing and defence procurement”.
Evan Feigenbaum of the Carnegie Endowment for International Peace described the strategy in a post on social media as showing “a lot of preening about American primacy”, adding that it “will leave many in Asia cold at a time when the US has withdrawn from rule setting, refused to forge (much less actually join) regional trade pacts, calls Asian allies predators, and treats allies largely as instrumental players in a US proxy competition with China”.
The strategy also highlights specific regional groupings. Farwa Aamer of the Asia Society noted in a statement that the strategy “throws a lifeline” to the Quadrilateral Security Dialogue or Quad, the four-nation security bloc led by the US that includes Japan, Australia and India, aimed at ensuring a free and open Indo-Pacific.
A leaders’ summit of the bloc was scheduled this fall in India but was derailed after Washington and New Delhi failed to strike a trade deal. The South China Morning Post previously reported that Trump is unlikely to visit India without a favourable trade pact.
In Friday’s document, the US calls on India to contribute more to Indo-Pacific security and work with other allies “in preventing domination by any single competitor nation” and to “enlist” New Delhi, among other allies, “to cement and improve our joint positions in the Western Hemisphere and, with regard to critical minerals, in Africa”.
The transactional approach is not limited to Asia.
The document also scrutinises US allies in Europe. Ian Bremmer, president of the Eurasia Group, noted on social media that the language “reflects an administration that believes a weak EU is in America’s interests”.
The strategy criticises Europe’s migration and defence policies and suggests that the US should prioritise “cultivating resistance to Europe’s current trajectory within European nations”.
It warns that “their economic decline is eclipsed only by the real and stark prospect of civilizational erasure. If present trends continue, the continent will be unrecognisable in 20 years”, underscoring a strategic scepticism towards even America’s oldest allies.
Trump’s strategy plan has also drawn immediate domestic criticism.
Representative Gregory Meeks, ranking member of the House Foreign Affairs Committee, condemned the document in a statement as emblematic of a “cynical, dog-eat-dog philosophy” and said it signals to allies that “the US is no longer a reliable partner”.
Meeks, a Democrat from New York, argued that the document “discards decades of values-based US leadership in favour of a craven, unprincipled world view”.
He added that the administration’s “America first” framing “is not a strategy” and “disregards the country’s democratic values, network of like-minded allies and multilateral institutions that have historically supported US global leadership”.

data.title: Hong Kong’s hottest new openings, from a quiet coffee shop in Central to a Yunnan cuisine bistro
data.description:
Jija by Vicky Lau

Chef Vicky Lau of Tate Dining Room and Mora trades fine dining for the rustic, convivial spirit of southwest China at Jija. Found in the newly opened Kimpton hotel, the 50-seat bistro – named for the sound of cheerful chatter – focuses on Yunnan province’s robust flavours and famed produce, from wild mushroom salads to Puer tea-smoked chicken. The intimate space, inspired by Miao weaving, features a dedicated tea station and a striking wall of house-made pickles.
15/F, Kimpton Hotel, 11 Middle Road, Tsim Sha Tsui
Moonary Coffee

Tucked away on Central’s steep Aberdeen Street, Moonary Coffee is a quiet stop for those navigating the slopes between Sheung Wan and PMQ. The compact, minimalist cafe serves espresso-based drinks, matcha lattes and homestyle eats such as brownies, banana bread and yogurt bowls, while more adventurous drinkers can opt for its signature tomato-milk-washed coffee.
G/F, 25 Aberdeen Street, Central
Friday After Class

New to the Peel Street ruckus, Friday After Class offers a nostalgic two-part escape. Start on the terrace for tap cocktails (such as Boozy Boba and Fernet with Coke) and people-watching. Then, head inside to the intimate Common Room where head bartender Sabin Thapa presents a cocktail menu themed as a “sketchbook” of life chapters, moving from a plum-salt Bye-Bye Yuzu to an earthy, umami Shroom-Fashioned.
52 Peel Street, Central
Carmela

The team behind Francis brings their Mediterranean touch to Carmela, an all-day retreat on Des Voeux Road Central. From Coffeelin coffee and easy lunches to shared dinners, the coastal, ingredient-driven menu features whipped mackerel tarama and house-made pasta dishes such as Sardinian culurgiones. The minimal, white interior and concise cocktail list – including an olive oil martini – complete the sunlit, lived-in feel.
Shop G06, Nan Fung Tower, 173 Des Voeux Road Central, Central
